questions.prompt: Why use `type` instead of `which` to check what a command does?

choices.text: `which` only checks the PATH for executables — it misses shell built-ins (cd, type), aliases, and functions. `type` reports all of these. Concrete example: `which cd` returns nothing or an obscure binary; `type cd` says 'cd is a shell builtin' — which explains why cd behaves differently than file commands. Aliases are particularly easy to miss: `which ls` might show /bin/ls, but if you've aliased ls to 'ls --color=auto -F', that's what actually runs. `type ls` shows the alias. When debugging 'why is this command doing something weird,' `type` is the first move; it tells you whether you're hitting a binary, builtin, alias, or function, each of which has different override mechanics.

questions.explanation: `type` reports built-ins, aliases, and functions too — `which` only finds PATH executables.

questions.prompt: You need to delete all .pyc files under your project. Fast and safe command?

choices.text: `find . -name '*.pyc' -delete` — finds all .pyc files recursively from the current directory and deletes them in one pass. `rm *.pyc` only matches the current directory (no recursion); rm with shell globbing also won't recurse without globstar enabled. Find's `-delete` is efficient (no exec subshell per file) and safe (only deletes regular files matching the pattern). For sanity, run `find . -name '*.pyc'` first WITHOUT the delete to preview what would be deleted. Same pattern works for any clean-up: tempfiles, old logs, node_modules debris. The `find ... -delete` idiom is your daily cleanup tool.
